story.title: The New School
story.description: Emma moves to a new town and starts at a new school. Follow her journey as she finds the courage to make new friends and face new challenges.
story.author: ReadWell

vocabulary.definition: a special event or time, often one that is celebrated
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Emma's birthday was a special occasion, even though she was far from her old friends.

vocabulary.definition: a person who is part of your family, such as an aunt, uncle, or cousin
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Emma's relatives came from Sheffield to celebrate her birthday.

vocabulary.definition: to come together in one place as a group
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The family gathered around the table and told stories from the past.

vocabulary.definition: to make something look more attractive by adding things to it
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Emma's mother decorated the table with yellow flowers for the occasion.

vocabulary.definition: something unexpected that happens and causes a feeling of wonder or pleasure
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Sophie's visit was a wonderful surprise that Emma did not expect at all.

grammarFocus.explanation: Some common verbs do not follow the regular -ed pattern in the simple past. They change their form completely. These are called irregular verbs and must be learned individually. They are very common in English stories and everyday speech.
examples: Emma's aunt and uncle came all the way from Sheffield.
Sophie stood at the door and gave Emma a handmade card.
Emma felt something she didn't expect — this was exactly right.
Her mother made the cake from her grandmother's recipe and took a photograph of everyone.
formGuide: Use one past form for all subjects, but irregular verbs do not follow the -ed rule.
Learn the common past forms as full word changes, like go becomes went and take becomes took.
Keep the irregular past form only in positive statements; use the base form after did in questions and negatives.
usageNotes: Use it for finished past events in stories and retelling.
Notice the most frequent verbs first because they appear again and again in reading.
grammarFocus.contrastWith: Do not add -ed to irregular verbs. Their past forms must be remembered as separate words.
commonMistakes: Inventing forms like buyed or taked.
Using the irregular past after did in a question or negative.
Mixing a present form and a past form in the same past-time sentence.

content.text: Emma's birthday fell on a Saturday in October. It should have felt like a special occasion, but she woke up late and lay in bed looking at the ceiling. Last year, her best friend Jade organised a surprise party in the garden. This year, Emma thought there would be no surprise. Her mother told her they would have a quiet lunch. Emma got up, went to the bathroom, and walked downstairs.

grammarAnnotations.note: went is the irregular past of go

content.text: But when she came into the kitchen, she saw that her mother had decorated the table with yellow flowers. There was a card and two extra chairs at the table. Emma's aunt and uncle sat in those chairs. They came from Sheffield and said nothing about coming in advance. "Happy birthday, Emma," her uncle said. He stood up and hugged her. Her aunt gave her a small box wrapped in blue paper. Emma did not know what to say, so she just sat down.

grammarAnnotations.note: came is the irregular past of come
stood is the irregular past of stand
gave is the irregular past of give

content.text: The whole family gathered around the table. Her uncle told stories Emma had never heard before about her mother when she was young and about a holiday where everything went wrong. Emma's mother laughed at things Emma had never seen her laugh at. It was a different kind of afternoon — the kind Emma knew she would remember.

grammarAnnotations.note: told is the irregular past of tell
went is the irregular past of go
knew is the irregular past of know

content.text: At two o'clock, the doorbell rang. Emma's mother went to the door and came back with Sophie. Sophie stood in the hallway with a wrapped gift and a card she made herself. "I hope this is okay," she said. "Your mum told me it was your birthday." Emma took the gift and felt something she did not know how to name. It was a real surprise. "Come in," Emma said. Sophie gave her a big smile and came inside.

grammarAnnotations.note: went is the irregular past of go
felt is the irregular past of feel
gave is the irregular past of give

content.text: Her mother made the cake from a recipe that came from Emma's grandmother. Her uncle took a photograph of everyone at the table. Later, Emma thought about how different the day felt. She thought she knew what a birthday away from home would feel like. She was wrong. That evening she wrote to Jade in Bristol. She told her about her relatives, about Sophie at the door, and about the cake. She wrote: "It was different from last year. But it was good." Then she sent it.

grammarAnnotations.note: made is the irregular past of make
took is the irregular past of take
wrote is the irregular past of write

comprehension.question: Why did Emma not expect a celebration on her birthday?
options: She told her mother she did not want a party.
Her mother said they would only have a quiet lunch, just the three of them.
Her relatives were too far away to visit.
She forgot her own birthday.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Her mother said they would only have a quiet lunch, just the three of them.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 1 says: 'Her mother told her they would have a quiet lunch, just the three of them.' This is why Emma expected nothing special.

comprehension.question: Which of the following uses an irregular past tense verb correctly?
options: Emma's uncle taked a photograph of everyone.
Sophie gived Emma a handmade card.
Emma's aunt and uncle came from Sheffield by train.
Her mother maked the cake from a family recipe.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Emma's aunt and uncle came from Sheffield by train.
comprehension.explanation: 'Came' is the correct irregular past form of 'come.' The other options use incorrect forms — 'taked', 'gived', and 'maked' are not real words. The correct forms are: took, gave, and made.

comprehension.question: Why was Sophie's visit a surprise?
options: Sophie arrived with a very expensive gift.
Emma's mother had arranged it secretly without telling Emma.
Sophie lived very far away and rarely visited.
Emma and Sophie had argued the day before.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Emma's mother had arranged it secretly without telling Emma.
comprehension.explanation: Sophie says 'Your mum told me it was your birthday', which means Emma's mother invited her. Emma didn't know — so it was a surprise arranged by her mother.

comprehension.question: What does the word 'gather' suggest in paragraph 3?
options: The family collected objects from around the house.
Everyone came together around the table as a group.
Emma's mother picked flowers from the garden.
The relatives took a long time to arrive.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Everyone came together around the table as a group.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 3 says 'The whole family gathered around the table' just before describing everyone telling stories together. This shows 'gather' means to come together in one place as a group.

options: Emma gived her friend a card and taked a photo.
Emma gave her friend a card and took a photo.
Emma gives her friend a card and took a photo.
Emma giving her friend a card and took a photo.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: Emma gave her friend a card and took a photo.
grammarPractice.explanation: Irregular past forms do not take -ed.

grammarPractice.prompt: Complete the sentence: Last night Emma ___ home late because of the party. (go)
grammarPractice.explanation: The simple past of go is went.

grammarPractice.prompt: Correct the sentence: She buyed a gift for her friend.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: She bought a gift for her friend.
grammarPractice.explanation: The simple past of buy is bought.

grammarPractice.prompt: Rewrite in the simple past: He takes the bus to school.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: He took the bus to school.
grammarPractice.explanation: The simple past of take is took.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the sentence that correctly retells Emma's birthday surprise.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: Sophie's gift came at the right moment and made Emma smile.
grammarPractice.explanation: Came and made are the correct irregular and past forms for the story event.
options: Sophie's gift come at the right moment and maked Emma smile.
Sophie's gift came at the right moment and made Emma smile.
Sophie's gift came at the right moment and make Emma smile.
Sophie's gift was coming at the right moment and made Emma smile.

vocabulary.definition: a short performance to show your skills, used to decide if you can join a play, film, or group
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Sophie and Emma decided to go to the audition for the school play together.

vocabulary.definition: to practise a performance, such as a play or a speech, before the real event
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Emma planned to rehearse her lines every evening before the audition.

vocabulary.definition: to do something such as acting, singing, or dancing in front of an audience
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Emma was nervous about having to perform in front of the whole school.

vocabulary.definition: the group of people who watch or listen to a performance
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Emma imagined standing on the stage in front of a large audience of parents and students.

vocabulary.definition: a character that an actor plays in a film or play
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Sophie was hoping to get a role in the school play, and she thought Emma could too.

grammarFocus.explanation: We use 'going to' for plans and intentions — things we have already decided to do. We use 'will' for predictions, spontaneous decisions, and things we are not yet certain about. Both talk about the future, but the choice depends on whether the decision is already made or still open.
examples: I'm going to audition for the school play on Thursday. (decided plan)
I think it will be fine. (prediction about the future)
I won't know the result until next week. (future uncertainty)
You're probably going to forget the words the first time. (prediction based on experience)
formGuide: Use be going to + base verb for plans and intentions already decided.
Use will + base verb for predictions, promises, and quick decisions.
Keep be with going to: am going to, is going to, are going to.
usageNotes: Use going to when the speaker already has a plan in mind.
Use will when the future idea is a prediction or a decision made in the moment.
grammarFocus.contrastWith: Both forms talk about the future, but they are not identical. Going to sounds planned; will often sounds spontaneous or uncertain.
commonMistakes: Using will when the sentence clearly describes a prior plan.
Adding another future marker after will, like 'will going to'.

content.text: On a Monday in November, Sophie put a yellow flyer on Emma's desk. "The school play," Sophie said. "Mr Hughes is holding auditions next Thursday. I'm going to try for it, and you should come too." Emma looked at the flyer. The school play was going to be a version of A Midsummer Night's Dream. She had seen the film once but did not understand most of it. "I'm not an actor," Emma said. Sophie smiled. "Neither am I," she said.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: I'm going to try for it

content.text: Sophie ignored every reason Emma gave. "I'm going to help you practise," she said. "We're going to do it together. It's going to be fine." Emma was not certain it was going to be fine. She thought about standing on a stage in front of an audience. "What if I forget the words?" she said. "You probably will," Sophie said cheerfully. "But you won't forget all of them. And Mr Hughes won't mind."

grammarAnnotations.targetText: I'm going to help you practise
We're going to do it together

content.text: That evening, Emma decided she was going to try. She wrote a list in her notebook: she was going to read the scenes that night, she was going to rehearse her lines every morning, and she was going to ask her mother to listen to her perform before Thursday. Her mother was very enthusiastic. She sat down in the kitchen and became the most encouraging audience Emma had ever had. She clapped after every line.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: was going to rehearse her lines
was going to ask her mother

content.text: On Thursday, Emma and Sophie waited outside the drama room. Mr Hughes appeared at the door. "Two today," he said, looking at them both. "Good. One at a time, please — Sophie first, Emma second." Sophie gave Emma a quick thumbs-up and went in. Emma stood in the corridor and thought: it will be over soon, and whatever happens, it will be finished. This thought was more helpful than she expected.

An hour later, Emma and Sophie sat on the bench outside school and waited for Emma's mother's car. "How do you think it went?" Sophie asked. Emma considered this carefully. "I won't know for certain until the list goes up," she said. "But I didn't forget all the words." Sophie nodded. "I think you're going to get a role," she said. Emma looked at her. "I think you probably will too," she said. Next week, they would find out.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: you're going to get a role

comprehension.question: What is the difference between these two sentences from the story: 'I'm going to help you practise' and 'It will be fine'?
options: The first is in the present tense and the second is in the past tense.
'Going to' describes a plan already decided; 'will' is a prediction about the future.
Both sentences mean the same thing.
'Going to' is used for other people's plans; 'will' is used for your own.
comprehension.correctAnswer: 'Going to' describes a plan already decided; 'will' is a prediction about the future.
comprehension.explanation: This is the key grammar point of this chapter. 'I'm going to help you' shows a decision Sophie has already made. 'It will be fine' is Sophie's prediction about an uncertain future event. This matches the grammar focus explanation.

comprehension.question: Why does Emma decide to try out for the play, even though she said she was 'not an actor'?
options: Mr Hughes asked her personally to audition.
She wanted to impress her mother.
Sophie encouraged her and she decided to try despite her doubts.
She found out the play would not be performed in front of an audience.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Sophie encouraged her and she decided to try despite her doubts.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 3 says 'Emma decided she was going to try.' The story shows that Sophie's encouragement — and her refusal to accept Emma's reasons not to try — was what changed Emma's mind.

comprehension.question: What does 'audience' mean in paragraph 2?
options: The actors who perform in the play
The group of people who watch a performance
The judge who decides who gets a role
The students who auditioned for the play
comprehension.correctAnswer: The group of people who watch a performance
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes Emma imagining 'standing on a stage in front of an audience of parents and teachers and every single student in the school.' All those people would be watching — not performing. This shows that an audience is the group who watches.

comprehension.question: At the end of the story, Emma says 'I won't know for certain until the list goes up.' Which grammar form is this?
options: Going to — it describes a plan she has already made.
Will — it describes a future situation she is not yet certain about.
Simple present — it describes a habit.
Past tense — it describes something that already happened.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Will — it describes a future situation she is not yet certain about.
comprehension.explanation: 'I won't know' uses 'will' in the negative. Emma is talking about a future result (the cast list) that has not been decided yet. She is not making a plan — she is describing an uncertain future state, which is a key use of 'will'.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the sentence that shows a plan.
options: We will practise after school because we arranged it yesterday.
We are going to practise after school because we arranged it yesterday.
We going to practise after school because we arranged it yesterday.
We are going practise after school because we arranged it yesterday.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: We are going to practise after school because we arranged it yesterday.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use be going to for plans made before the moment of speaking.

grammarPractice.prompt: Complete the sentence: Do not worry, I ___ help you with the costumes.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use will for a decision or promise made at the moment of speaking.

grammarPractice.prompt: Correct the sentence: Look at those dark clouds! It is going rain.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: Look at those dark clouds! It is going to rain.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use going to for a prediction based on evidence.

grammarPractice.prompt: Rewrite using going to: We planned a picnic for Saturday.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: We are going to have a picnic on Saturday.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use be going to for a future plan that already exists.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the best future sentence about Emma's audition plan and Sophie's prediction.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: Emma is going to rehearse tonight, and Sophie thinks she will get a role.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use going to for Emma's plan and will for Sophie's prediction.
options: Emma will rehearse tonight, and Sophie thinks she is going to get a role.
Emma is going to rehearse tonight, and Sophie thinks she will get a role.
Emma is rehearse tonight, and Sophie thinks she will gets a role.
Emma is going to rehearse tonight, and Sophie thinks she going to get a role.
